content.full_text:
California
(‘
Fair Political
.
Practices Commission
May 5,
1994
Jo Seibert
City Clerk
City of Vista
600 Eucalyptus Avenue
Post Office Box 1988
Vista,
CA 92085
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A—94—032
Dear Ms. Seibert:
This is in response to your letter requesting advice on
behalf of the various advisory commissions of the City Council of
the City of Vista regarding its high school student members’
responsibilities, if any, under the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”).
1
OUESTIONS
1.
Are Vista High School students who serve on various city
council advisory commissions as nonvoting members required to file
Statements of Economic Interests?
2.
If the answer to the above is in the affirmative, are
these students required to disclose the economic interests of
their parents?
CONCLUSIONS
1.
and 2.
Vista High School students are not public
officials under the Act; therefore, they are not required to file
Statements of Economic Interests.
FACTS
The City Council of the City of Vista appoints citizens to
serve on various advisory commissions.
Senior high school
students are encouraged to serve on each of the commissions.
If
enough interest is generated among the students,
two students are
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections
18000—18954.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No. A-94-032
Page 2
appointed to each commission.
The students are allowed a
nonbinding advisory vote on each motion.
ANALYSIS
In 1980, the Commission was asked whether nonvoting, student
members of the Boards of Trustees of Community College Districts
who were appointed pursuant to Education Code Section 72023.5 were
public officials.
2
The Commission concluded that these particular
students should file Statements of Economic Interests.
(Memorandum A-80-077.)
Though the student members were not “making decisions” within
the meaning of the Act, the Commission reasoned they were
“participating in making decisions.”
“Participating in making
decisions” includes,
in part:
(2)
Advises or makes recommendations to the
decision-maker, either directly or without
significant intervening substantive review,
by:
(B)
Preparing or presenting any report,
analysis or opinion, orally or in writing, which
requires the exercise of judgment on the part of
the official or designated employee and the purpose
of which is to influence the decision.
(Regulation 18700(c) (2).)
The purpose of having the student members serve on the
governing boards of community college districts was to advise the
boards, with the intent of influencing their decisions,
by
presenting the students’ opinions, or, at least, the opinion of
the student member.
Based on the information you provided,
none of the factors
present in the community college boards of trustees situation
appear to be present here.
First, Vista senior high school
students are not appointed to various city council advisory
commissions pursuant to a state statute or a municipal ordinance.
Rather, they volunteer.
Second, the purpose of having the student
members on these numerous advisory commissions is to further their
education, not to advise the commission and influence its
decisions.
2
Education Code Section 72023.5 provides,
in pertinent part,
that the students are recognized as full members of the board at
meetings,
including receiving all materials presented to the board
members and participating in the questioning of witnesses and the
discussion of issues.
However, the student members are nonvoting
and are not included in determining the vote required to vary any
measure before the board.


Our File No.
A—94—032
Page
3
For the above reasons, I advised you on March 15,
1994, that
it does not appear that Vista high school students are public
officials under the Act
3 and therefore they are not required to
file Statements of Economic Interests.
If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at
(916)
322—59O1.
Sincerely,
Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel
By:
Deanne Stone
Senior Commission Counsel
“Public official” means every natural person who is a member,
officer,
employee or consultant of a state or local government
agency.
(Section 82048.)
“Member” includes, but is not limited
to, salaried or unsalaried members of boards or coimuissions with
decisionmaking authority.
(Regulation 18700(a) (1).)
Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.
Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10c per page.

their parents?

Statements of Economic Interests.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1.
Are Vista High School students who serve on various city
council advisory commissions as nonvoting members required to file
Statements of Economic Interests?
2.
If the answer to the above is in the affirmative, are
these students required to disclose the economic interests of
their parents?

CONCLUSION: 1.
and 2.
Vista High School students are not public
officials under the Act; therefore, they are not required to file
Statements of Economic Interests.
